translatedText: English painter and poet William Blake born (November 28).
Became an engraver's apprentice at the age of 14.
Married at the age of 25.
Published Poetical Sketches.
Published Songs of Innocence.
Published the illustrated poetry collection The Marriage of Heaven and Hell.
Published the illustrated poetry collection Songs of Experience.
Published Illustrations of the Book of Job, adding mystical speculation to the Christian Bible story.
Attempted 100 illustrations for Dante's Divine Comedy, but left it unfinished.
William Blake died (August 12).
Activities as a Poet and Painter
Active as a major English artist, publishing poems and paintings intertwined with mystery and fantasy.
Production of Original Illustrated Poetry Collections
Published unique illustrated poetry collections by inventing relief etching (illuminated printing) to add illustrations to every page.
Publication of Major Works
Left several famous illustrated poetry collections, including The Marriage of Heaven and Hell (1790), Songs of Experience (1794), and Illustrations of the Book of Job (1825).
Establishing the Artistic Status of Illustrations
Evaluated as a figure who raised the perception of illustrations to be on par with other paintings.
What was William Blake's profession?
He was an English painter and poet.
What kind of painting did William Blake despise?
He despised paintings that merely copied the external appearance of nature, such as portraits or landscapes.
What was William Blake's preferred painting medium?
He avoided oil painting and considered watercolor to be the best medium of expression.
What are William Blake's representative illustrated poetry collections?
His representative illustrated poetry collections include The Marriage of Heaven and Hell (1790), Songs of Experience (1794), and Illustrations of the Book of Job (1825).
How did William Blake produce his illustrated poetry collections?
He published illustrated poetry collections by adding illustrations to every page and inventing his own relief etching method for color printing.
April 2, 1805
Born in Odense, Denmark.
April 15, 1805
Baptized in St. Hans Church, Odense.
His father died.
His mother remarried.
Published his first story, The Ghost at Palnatoke's Grave.
November 1823
His birth certificate was drawn up.
Attended school in Elsinore as well.
Achieved considerable success with the short story A Journey on Foot from Holmen's Canal to the East Point of Amager.
Experimented with The Travelling Companion.
Published Shadow Pictures of a Journey to the Harz, Swiss Saxony, etc. in the Summer of 1831, a collection of travel sketches.
Received a small travel stipend from the king and began his first journey traveling across Europe.
October 1834
Arrived in Rome.
His first novel, The Improvisatore, was published and received immediate acclaim.
Published the first installment of Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.
December 16, 1835
Published the second booklet of Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.
Danish reviews of the first two booklets appeared for the first time.
April 7, 1837
Published the third booklet of Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.
Began his second series of fairy tales.
Published Picture Book Without Pictures.
Jenny Lind wrote a letter regarding Andersen as a "brother."
Began his third series of fairy tales.
Lived at Nyhavn 67 in Copenhagen.
June 1847
Visited England for the first time and met Charles Dickens.
Published In Sweden, a collection of travel sketches.
Visited England again, primarily to meet Dickens.
March 1862
Mentioned this period in his diary as his "erotic time."
November 13, 1863
Wrote in his diary, "Scharff has not visited me in eight days; with him it is over."
Published A Visit to Portugal in 1866.
Received an offer for 12 new stories from Horace Scudder, editor of the Riverside Magazine for Young People.
Early 1872
Fell out of bed at the age of 67 and was severely injured.
August 4, 1875
Died at the age of 70 at Rolighed near Copenhagen.
The Collin family grave was moved to another cemetery where younger Collin family members were buried.
Fame as a Fairy Tale Writer
Although he was a prolific writer of plays, travelogues, novels, and poems, he is best known for his fairy tales.
Prolific Writing and Translation
His 156 fairy tales are collected in 9 volumes and have been translated into more than 125 languages.
Cultural Impact
His fairy tales are deeply ingrained in the collective consciousness of Western culture.
Success of A Journey on Foot from Holmen's Canal to the East Point of Amager
Achieved considerable success with his 1829 short story A Journey on Foot from Holmen's Canal to the East Point of Amager.
Publication of The Improvisatore
His first novel, The Improvisatore, published in 1835, received immediate acclaim.
Publication of Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.
Ventured into the fairy tale genre with this collection, published in serial format between 1835 and 1837.
Establishing International Fame with The Little Mermaid
The Little Mermaid, included in the third booklet, established Andersen's international fame.
Publication of Travelogues
Published several long travelogues, such as In Sweden (1851) and A Poet's Bazaar, which were well received.
Publication of Picture Book Without Pictures
Achieved good results with Picture Book Without Pictures, published in 1840.
International Acclaim
Received acclaim across Europe beginning with his 1838 and 1845 fairy tale series.
What is Hans Christian Andersen best known for as a writer?
Although he was a prolific writer of plays, travelogues, novels, and poems, he is best known for his fairy tales.
Into how many languages have Andersen's fairy tales been translated?
His 156 fairy tales, spanning 9 volumes, have been translated into more than 125 languages.
What are Andersen's most famous fairy tales?
His most famous fairy tales include The Emperor's New Clothes, The Red Shoes, The Little Match Girl, The Ugly Duckling, The Little Mermaid, Thumbelina, and The Snow Queen.
When and where was Andersen born?
Andersen was born in Odense, Denmark, on April 2, 1805.
What kind of people were Andersen's parents?
His father had a primary education, and his mother was an illiterate washerwoman.
Did Andersen ever meet Charles Dickens?
Yes, Andersen first met Charles Dickens during a visit to England in June 1847, and visited England again in 1857 primarily to meet him.
When did Andersen die?
Andersen died on August 4, 1875, at the age of 70, at Rolighed near Copenhagen.
How was Andersen's childhood reflected in his works?
The Little Match Girl was based on his impoverished mother, The Snow Queen on his father's death, and The Ugly Duckling on the mistreatment he received because of his background.
December 30, 1865
Born in Bombay, India.
Returned to India.
Served as an editor for the Civil & Military Gazette and Pioneer in Lahore.
Published the poetry collection Departmental Ditties.
Published the short story collection Plain Tales from the Hills.
